I hear both sides, and there is merit in each.
Where we all agree is a healthy company in a year.
What if we funded one region and reviewed it at half-year?
Building on Adriana point, we could cap the spend.
Can everyone live with that, or is it just a lukewarm yes?
Victor, I will be blunt: Operations cannot back funding four regions. The margin is too thin this year.
I hear you, and there is real merit in that. Protecting the margin is not caution, it is responsibility. Growth has a point too, but I am not going to pretend the risk is not real.
I appreciate that. But we keep landing in a stalemate, your team pushes, mine holds, and nothing moves.
Then let us start from what we share. Where we all agree is a healthy company in eighteen months, not just a clean quarter. If that is the real goal, we are closer than it looks.
Agreed on the goal. So what is the actual proposal, and do not just tell me to approve it.
What if we funded one region now instead of four, and reviewed it at the half-year? That is the middle ground: growth gets traction, your margin stays protected.
That I can work with. If you would cap the spend, I would stop fighting the whole thing.
Building on your point, let us cap it and hold the review date firm. Before we lock it, can everyone genuinely live with this, a real yes, not a lukewarm one?
Yes, a real one. Capped, one region, reviewed at half-year. That is a win-win I will defend to my team.
We were in a stalemate until the two factions began to converge on a shared goal.
Each side conceded a little, found the middle ground, and reached a real win-win.
Building on your point, we could cap the spend.
Can everyone genuinely live with that, not a lukewarm yes?

Right, the leadership meeting is in twenty minutes and the room is split down the middle. Operations wants to protect this year's margin; growth wants to fund the new regions now. Two factions, both with a point, and if I just push my side we end in a stalemate. So here is my plan to broker it. First, I acknowledge both sides honestly before I argue anything. Operations is right that the margin matters, and growth is right that we cannot stall. If people feel heard, they give ground; if they feel run over, they dig in. Second, I name the common ground out loud, because there always is some. We all want the company healthy in eighteen months, not just this quarter. Starting from what we share lowers the temperature. Third, I propose with hypothetical language, not orders. What if we funded one region now and reviewed it at the half-year? Suppose we capped the spend so the margin holds. That invites people in instead of forcing a yes. Fourth, I build on whatever good point someone makes rather than overriding it. And last, before we move on, I check that everyone can genuinely live with it, not a lukewarm shrug but a real yes. A decision nobody owns falls apart by Friday. Acknowledge, find the middle ground, propose softly, and confirm. That is how you turn a divided room into a win-win.

Hi Victor, it's Adriana. A quick word before you walk into that leadership meeting, because aligning a divided room is a real skill and most people get it wrong. Three things. First, do not start by selling your answer. Start by making both factions feel understood. Say it plainly: there's merit in both positions. The moment people feel heard, they stop defending and start listening, and they will concede far more than you expect. Skip this and the room stays in a stalemate, no matter how good your idea is. Second, build the decision from the common ground up, not from your conclusion down. Name what everyone already agrees on, then move toward the middle ground from there. People reconcile around shared goals, not around your slide. Third, and this is where strong managers slip, propose tentatively and then check for real buy-in. Use what if and suppose and I'd suggest, so it feels like a shared idea, not a verdict. Then close the loop: can everyone live with this? If you only get a lukewarm nod, you do not have consensus, you have a problem that comes back next week. A real yes from the whole room is worth more than a brilliant plan half the room resents. Acknowledge, converge, propose, confirm. You will get them there.
